texts.full_text: for European Studies About People Research Grants Academics Events News & Media Home Search Menu Events Home › Events › Digitizing Imperial Rome: A computerized approach to the Architectural History of the Roman Imperial Forum Digitizing Imperial Rome: A computerized Approach to the Architectural History of the Roman Imperial Forum Time: Mon Oct 11, 2010, 4:30 pm - 5:45 pm Location: 114 Bond Hall, School of Architecture Althoughdigital approaches are already being applied in a wide range of scientific disciplines and humanities fields (from astronomy to musicology), their use is less prevalent within the domain of architectural history. This paper seeks not only provide an overview on how different aspects of ancient architecture can be explored by computerized methods – such as spatial analysis, statistical comparisons or simulation techniques -, but also presents several examples in which digital technology provides innovative tools for research and publication. In particular a recent study concerning Rome's Imperial forum complex will show the way these analytical approaches are being employed. This work seeks to shed light on how ancient authors depicted their view of what they perceived as “Imperial” architecture by tracing the development phases over time from Augustus’ foundation in 27 B.C.E., until his grandnephew Commodus terminated any further construction activities during early third century C.E. By developing and utilizing three-dimensional computer models for specific points within this chronological framework, an innovative view on Rome's Forum complex was obtained. This perspective not only allows to follow the changes of space in time – from Augustan architectural order through a more informal “Hellenistic” approach under Trajan - but also presents new means (and limitations) with which we can examine architecture

Women Hear from Doctors A new book exposes how women still can’t get equal medical care. Kate Harloe Bio Share on Facebook Share on Twitter Email Print Comments Mother Jones illustration Fight disinformation. Get a daily recap of the facts that matter. Sign up for the free Mother Jones newsletter. In the early 1960s, researchers noticed that women seemed to have lower rates of heart disease than men—but only until menopause, when women’s estrates started catching up and surpassing those in middle-aged women by some measures. This observation prompted a bit more research into how gender impacts health outcomes. Also, as the article goes on to discuss through examples from Dr. Sabrina Strings' book "Femmes Fatales: How Black Women Are Dying for Medicine's Fixation with Biology," women often encounter dismissive or degrading stereotypes when seeking medical care from different conditions and demographics—especially in gynecology. The piece highlights cases where female patients report symptoms that are downplayed, dismissed as mental rather than physiological (like menstrual pain being linked to stress), ignored altogether ("You have to calm down"), leading some people into delayed treatment for serious health problems such as endometriosis or ovarian cancer. It further delves into how these patterns disproportionately affect marginalized communities of color and lower socioeconomic backgrounds where systemic racism affects unequal access not only in healthcare quality but also the willingness among providers sometimes seen towards addressing their concerns. texts.full_text: 4 You Family and marriage Pre-Marriage Counselling Sex & intimacy Infidelity recovery counselling Divorce Counselling Retreats Intensives Babysleepfairy Corporate solutions Contact Dealing with Depression: From a holistic point of view Home Articles Anxiety and depression ﻿ Dealing with Depression: From a holistic point of view Dealing with Depression: From a holistic point of view The term depression is batted about very freely by the population at large and the media. In fact, many clinical terms of psychology and mental health have made their way into peoples’ everyday use anD thus changing the meaning of those words from what they represent clinically. Depression, for instance, is often used to describe a low mood, a bad day, or a temporary sadness, which Is far from the clinical and often debilitating condition of major depressive disorder. However, for those who truly suffer from clinical depression, the condition is a far cry from the colloquial use of the term. It is a pervasive and often debilitating condition, characterized by a persistent feeling of sadness and a lack of interest in previously enjoyable activities. It can lead to a range of emotional and physical problems and can significantly reduce a person's ability to function at work and at home. In the holistic approach to dealing with depression, the focus is on treating the person, not the disorder. It is a comprehensive approach that takes into account the physical, emotional, and spiritual aspects of a person's life. It is a departure from the traditional, purely medical approach that relies on medication and therapy to treat depression. A holistic approach to treating depression might include a variety of therapies and treatments, including: 1. Lifestyle changes, such as a healthy diet, regular exercise, and adequate sleep, as well as the avoidance of drugs and alcohol, can all contribute to a holistic approach to treating depression. 2. texts.full_text: Duke chatted to the crew of Scotland's Charity Air Ambulance (SCAA). William was also shown inside the aircraft and talked through its workings as he expressed a keen interest in the operation. The encounter came days after reports suggested William, who stepped down as a search-and-rescue pilot with the RAF last year, is considering taking a role as an air ambulance pilot. William reportedly told aides he is not ready to take on full-time royal engagements and plans are said to be under way for him to work with the East Anglian Air Ambulance service. He is said to have made no mention of his future plans when he met the charity ambulance crew. John Pritchard, 46, lead paramedic with the SCAA, said: "He never said a thing about it, but I would certainly welcome him on to the team. "He is absolutely passionate about his flying and you can see he's a really caring person. I would imagine he would be keen to keep that going." Mr Pritchard said the Duke was "really keen" to talk to the crew. "He asked some really good questions about the aircraft and about what patients we attend," the paramedic said. "About 60% of our call-outs are to trauma patients, motorcycle RTCs, to equestrian or off road cycle (accidents). "He was really keen, he asked us some fantastic questions about aviation. It was really good engaging with him. His knowledge was superb. "He wanted to have a quick look inside and see how it's all fitted. texts.full_text: Dissociation is a mental process where an individual disconnects from their thoughts, feelings, memories, or sense of identity. This dissociation can impact the sensory status, which refers to an individual's perception and interpretation of sensory stimuli. When a person experiences dissociation, their sensory processing can be altered. Studies havesuggested that dissociation may lead to a decrease in sensory sensitivity and an altered sense of body ownership. This means that individuals may feel detached from their senses, including touch, sight, sound, taste, and smell. The use of tools like Bagolini striated lenses and the Worth 4 Dot test can help assess sensory integration and visual processing. These tests provide insights into eye alignment, binocular vision, and the ability to maintain fusion, which is the brain's process of forming a single image from the slightly different images seen by each eye. by combining the understanding of dissociation and sensory status, researchers and clinicians can develop therapeutic approaches to address sensory processing disorders, improve visual-motor skills, and enhance overall sensory integration for individuals who have experienced dissociation. Choose Wisely: Select an activity that genuinely interests you. It could be anything from painting, playing music instruments (like the guitar), coding, cooking exotic cuisines or even collecting stamps. 2. Start Small and Simple: - For instance: + If it's photography: Start with your phone camera before investing in a DSLR. + With knitting/crocheting: Begin by making simple scarves instead of complex sweaters right away. + In woodworking, start small projects like birdhouses or picture frames. 3. Set Aside Time: Dedicate regular time slots for practicing and honing your new skill/hobby to make it a habit that sticks around long-term (e.g., 1 hour every Sunday afternoon). 4. Learn from Others: - Join clubs, classes, workshops related to the hobby. - Watch tutorials on YouTube or follow along with online courses/tutorials tailored for beginners. 5. Be Patient and Persistent: Remember that everyone starts as a beginner! Don't rush progress; enjoy each step of learning something new while being patient when facing challenges/difficulties common in any pursuit worth having! 6. Invest Wisely: - Only buy necessary equipment once you're sure about your commitment to the hobby. - Look for affordable alternatives or used items initially. 7. Make Friends: Connect with others who share similar interests through online forums, social media groups (e.g., Facebook), local clubs/meetups etc.; they can provide support and motivation while making new friends! 8. In the field of organic chemistry it is often desirable to perform a variety of related chemical reactions simultaneously under similar reacting conditions. The technique for performing such reactions simultaneously is known as parallel synthesis. One of the problems associated with carrying out parallel syntheses in the laboratory is that the majority of existing laboratory magnetic stirrers are only designcapable so operate two or more single reaction vessels per unit at any given moment, and there must be a considerable distance between stirring units. Even when employing such systems to perform multiple responses under different reaction circumstances it can prove very costly not impossible. However there is an ongoing need in the art for improved laboratory magnetic stirrers suitable of use with parallel syntheses involving several reaction vessels per unit device so that each vessel, upon being positioned within a predetermined area capable effective stirring by such systems simultaneously. This will allow simultaneous synthesis under different reacting circumstances whilst creating considerable space and number of equipment. texts.full_text: about being great, but the willingness to learn is the key of succcess. In today's fast-paced world where knowledge evolves at an unprecedented rate and skills can become outdated overnight due to technological advancements or shifts in industry trends. It takes courage for individuals—be they students just beginning their academic journey, professionals seeking further specialization within a field of work that has already chosen them as experts by default; or retirees who wish not only to enjoy the fruits but also strive onward from life's harvest. The readiness and openness one must have in facing such challenges is indeed praiseworthy. The willingness isn't merely about learning for its own sake, although this would be laudable enough—it’s more a mindset of constant adaptation with an undying curiosity that propels us towards becoming better versions ourselves each day. It's the kind where one does not just see themselves in terms of their current capabilities and knowledge but also as someone capable to grow beyond these limits, continually striving for improvement. Moreover this willingness is often what distinguishes between those who merely 'live' life—going through its motions passively until it comes a time that they must retire or when the world moves past them; versus individuals whose lives are defined by their active engagement with learning and self-improvement. It's not about how much you know, but rather your readiness to learn what is next. So while we can certainly celebrate moments of greatness—be it academic achievements at school for a student or professional milestones in one’s career; the true essence that drives these accomplishments forward lies within each person and their willingness. It's this very spirit which has been instrumental throughout history, inspiring humanity to reach beyond its current state into greater heights. Description of Related Art RRecent advancements have introduced various innovations for reducing noise emissions from vehicle exhaust systems, especially focusing on low-frequency noises which are more challenging due to their penetrating nature that can disturb passengers as well as residents in surrounding areas when vehicles operate at certain engine speeds or conditions. In conventional dual-exhaust system designs without specific adaptations for CDA (Cylinder Deactivation) engines, the reduction of active cylinders during partial load operation leads to imbalanced exhaust pulses and resonance issues within one branch. This imbalance can cause significant low-frequency noise because deactivating a portion of an eight-cylinder engine effectively turns it into four or six cylinder mode in terms of its firing pattern. Therefore, there is a need for designing dual-exhaust systems specifically equipped with mechanisms that mitigate these effects during CDA operation to ensure smooth exhaust flow and reduced acoustic emissions. Solutions have been proposed such as variable geometry silencers which can alter their internal configuration based on the engine’s operating condition; however, they tend to be complex in structure or costly. texts.full_text: highly conserved neuropeptide. It is ubiquitously distributed, with highest levels found in the gastrointestinal tract and pituitary. Originally isolated from porcine spinal cord, it has since been isolated and sequenced from several species. Amino acid alignment of NmU from different species reveals a high level of conservation, and particular features within its structure are for bioactivity. Specifically, the C terminus, including a terminal asparagine-linked amidation, is essential for activity. The conservation of NmU across a wide range of species indicates a strong evolutionary pressure to conserve this peptide and points to its physiological significance. Despite this, the precise physiological and indeed pathophysiological roles of NmU have remained elusive. NmU was first isolated based on its ability to contract rat uterine smooth-muscle (hence the suffix "U") and has since been implicated in the regulation of smooth-muscle contraction, blood pressure and local blood flow, ion transport in the gut, stress responses, cancer, gastric acid secretion, pronociception, and feeding behavior. Two G-protein-coupled receptors for NmU have recently been cloned. These receptrors are related to each other but differ significantly from most of their closest known family members: Neuromedin B/Galanin (NMGB) type 1 receptor [NmUR(N)/galanoreceptor-like peptide, or GALPR; formerly galanobactorp/nociceptin/orphanins]. Interestingly a single endogenous NmU ligand has been identified for both the human receptors.

texts.full_text: 11, 15, and 20) were resistant to trimethoprim/sulphonamide, and had reduced susceptibility (but not clinical resistance) to sulphonamide compounds when compared to the *E. coli* EPI300 wild-type. Two clones (SUL3 and 5) were resistant to sulphonamide compounds and had reduced susceptibility (but not clinical resistance) to trimethoprim/sulphonamide compared to the EPI300 wild-type. The BACs from these nine clones were sequenced. The cloned DNA was taxonomically classified by sequence homology and gene synteny: four clones were identified as originating from *Neisseria subflava* (SUL6, SUL8, SUL9, and SUL15), four clones from *Veillonella parvula* (SUL3, SUL5, SUL10, and SUL20), and one clone from *Streptococcus infantis* (SUL11). The size of the inserts ranged from 10,250 bp to 21,161 bp and contained 11 to 20 predicted ORFs, summarised in [Table 2](#pone-0086428-t002){ref-type="table"}. All nine clones possessed the *folP* gene which encodes dihydropteroate synthase (DHPS). DHPS catalyses an essential step in the folic acid biosynthesis pathway and is the target of sulphonamide action [@pone.0086428-Skold1]. Certain mutant *folP* genes encode a DHPS enzyme that has a lower affinity for sulphonamides, and thus confer reduced susceptibility to this antibiotic. The predicted DHPS amino acid sequences from the BACs were aligned with DHPS sequences of representative *folP* genes (including sulphonamide susceptible and resistant variants) and analysed for the presence of mutations that can confer reduced susceptibility to sulphonamides ([Figure 1](#pone-0086428-g001){ref-type="fig"}). ![Alignment of the DHPS amino acid sequences from sulphonamide resistant BAC clones and representative DHPS sequences.\ The numbering
